summary:
The Battle of Sphacteria (425 BC) was a remarkable Athenian victory during the [[event:1010|Peloponnesian War]] in which a force of Athenian troops and light-armed allies surrounded and captured a garrison of Spartan soldiers on the island of Sphacteria off the southwest Peloponnese. The capture of approximately 120 Spartiates — full Spartan citizens — was a profound shock to Greek opinion, as Spartans were assumed to prefer death to surrender.

The operation grew from an Athenian general Demosthenes's opportunistic fortification of Pylos, a headland on the Messenian coast. When Sparta sent troops to dislodge him, they became trapped on the nearby island when the Athenian fleet arrived and cut off their retreat. A siege ensued until Demosthenes and the politician Cleon led a combined assault using archers, javelin throwers, and slingers who pelted the Spartans from a distance, negating their superiority in close combat. Facing annihilation, the remaining Spartans surrendered — an outcome that astonished all of Greece. The prisoners were held in Athens as hostages, deterring Spartan raids on Attica for years.
